mappings.value: . Here n is an integer Fourier mode index, b sub n is the coefficient of e to the i n theta in the perturbation shape mu, omega is the natural frequency variable, K is the coupling strength, and delta is the Kronecker delta: it equals one only when the two indices match and zero otherwise.
Equation 19 is Eq. 17 after using the periodicity in theta to expand the perturbation into Fourier modes.

The important structural point is that the mean-field feedback only touches the first harmonics n equals 1 and n equals minus 1. That is exactly what the Kronecker-delta factor delta n one plus delta n minus one enforces.

For all other nonzero modes, the right-hand side vanishes, leaving lambda plus i n omega equal to zero whenever b sub n is nonzero. This is why the paper immediately reads off the continuous spectrum lambda sub n of omega equals minus i n omega.

blocks.value: Conceptually, Eq. 19 decomposes the linear stability problem into Fourier channels. Instead of studying an arbitrary theta-dependent perturbation all at once, it asks what each circular wave number n does under the linearized dynamics.

This equation is used after Eq. 17 and Eq. 18 to understand the spectrum of the incoherent state. The first harmonics are special because they can change the order parameter; higher harmonics mostly phase-mix through the i n omega term.
